sections.heading: Why containers?
sections.content:
Problem: "It works on my machine" — different environments have different dependencies, OS versions, configurations. Solution: Package your application with its dependencies into a container that runs identically everywhere. 
Traditional:  App → depends on OS, JDK version, libraries, config → "works on dev, breaks on prod"
Containers:   App + runtime + dependencies → single image → runs same everywhere
Key distinction: • Image = immutable artifact containing application + runtime + immutable dependencies • Runtime environment = environment-specific configuration and secrets (injected at deploy time) Docker concepts: • Image: read-only template (like a class) — built from Dockerfile • Container: running instance of an image (like an object) • Registry: stores images (Docker Hub, company registry) • Dockerfile: recipe to build an image

sections.heading: Dockerfile for Spring Boot
sections.content:
# Multi-stage build — smaller final image
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk AS builder
WORKDIR /app

COPY .mvn/ .mvn/
COPY mvnw pom.xml ./
RUN ./mvnw dependency:go-offline

COPY src/ src/
RUN ./mvnw package -DskipTests

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
WORKDIR /app

RUN useradd --system --uid 10001 appuser
COPY --from=builder /app/target/delivery-service-*.jar app.jar

USER appuser
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]
Best practices: • Multi-stage: build with JDK, run with JRE only (smaller image) • Layer caching: copy pom.xml + wrapper first (dependencies cached), then src (changes frequently) • Non-root user: run as non-root for security • .dockerignore: exclude target/, .git/, IDE files Note on HEALTHCHECK: Docker's HEALTHCHECK directive is an option for standalone containers. In Kubernetes, probes are declared in the Pod spec instead — avoid duplicating both.

sections.heading: Docker Compose for local development
sections.content:
# docker-compose.yml — full local environment
services:
  delivery-service:
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:8080"]
    environment:
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: dev
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://db:5432/delivery
    depends_on: [db, kafka]

  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: delivery
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dev123
    ports: ["5432:5432"]

  kafka:
    image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.5.0
    ports: ["9092:9092"]
    environment:
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://kafka:9092
docker compose up → full stack running locally in seconds. Java EE comparison: Instead of installing WildFly + PostgreSQL + ActiveMQ manually, you define everything in one file and spin it up/down instantly.

sections.heading: Layers & Build Cache
sections.content:
Docker images are composed of layers. Each Dockerfile instruction creates a new layer. 
Layer 1: FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre     (base OS + JRE)
Layer 2: RUN useradd ...                  (create user)
Layer 3: COPY app.jar ...                 (your application)
Caching rules: • If a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached version • Once a layer changes, all subsequent layers are rebuilt • Order instructions from least-changing to most-changing Practical impact: • Dependencies change rarely → cache the dependency download step • Source code changes often → copy it last • This is why we copy pom.xml and run dependency:go-offline before copying src/

sections.heading: Service networking
sections.content:
Docker Compose creates a default network where services can reach each other by name: 
services:
  app:
    environment:
      DB_HOST: db          # ← service name = DNS hostname
  db:
    image: postgres:15
Key points: • Services communicate via service name (not IP) • Each service gets its own isolated network namespace • ports: exposes to the host; services on the same network don't need exposed ports to talk to each other

sections.heading: Volumes & persistence
sections.content:
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:   # named volume — persists across container restarts
Types: • Named volumes: managed by Docker, persist data (databases, uploads) • Bind mounts: map host directory into container (development hot-reload) 
# Bind mount for live code reload
services:
  frontend:
    volumes:
      - ./src:/app/src    # host ./src → container /app/src

sections.heading: Production considerations
sections.content: Docker Compose is primarily for local development and CI. For production orchestration, use Kubernetes (or similar). Key differences: • Compose: single host, no auto-healing, manual scaling • Kubernetes: multi-node, self-healing, auto-scaling, rolling updates Compose is still valuable for: • Local development environments • Integration test setups (e.g., Testcontainers) • CI pipelines that need a full stack
